text: role of frames, one of Bateson’s further contributions was also to analyse what sometimes went wrong in communication processes. In fact, his paper on play and fantasy was inserted in Part III of Steps to an Ecology of Mind, called Form and Pathology in Relationship. Bateson famously applied his approach to schizophrenic persons. Very often, argued Bateson, maladjusted individuals can be diagnosed as having lost the capability to identify meaningful frames. That is why they feel lost about information, which they cannot place and decode meaningfully. That is also why they often lose humour (jokes are essentially frame-plays). Bateson also extended this analysis of distorted communication to the level of larger social patterns and civilizational derailment. The observation has general relevance: once we lose meaningful frames to define the playful act, then it leads to an instant loss of the playful itself. Let us now apply these conceptual dimensions to a history of the present. Play and the De-civilizing Process: The Ludic Fall of the Roman Empire We live in a period with a craving for games and excitement. There are antecedents to our present malaise and here again a long term comparative framework can be instructive. Before approaching the role of game and gambling in the transition to modernity, let us briefly consider how one can interpret the ludic element of ancient Roman civilization. As Mumford (1961) argued, outlining this development serves Liminality and the Modern 152 to formulate some warning signs concerning cultural-political developments of relevance to the present. The notion of play is expressed in Latin with the word ludens, and from here Huizinga took his title. The word is almost certainly of Etruscan origin. During the history of Ancient Rome a development took place which is indeed startling. The ludi were public Roman religious festivals, started before 220 bc as annual communal games to give thanks to the gods. The ludi romani was a Roman version of the Greek Olympic Games. In general the Roman ludi were public games for the Roman population. The earliest ludi were chariot races, held to celebrate victories in war. Originally, all ludi seem to have been votive offerings (ludi votivi), staged as the fulfilment of a vow to a deity whose favour had been sought and evidenced. As the product of military victory, ludi were often connected to triumphs. The first recorded venatio (staged beast hunt) was presented in 186 bc by M. Fulvius Nobilior as part of his ludi votivi, for which he paid with booty displayed at his triumph. After the Roman Republic, and increasingly during the Roman Empire, the ludi included gladiatorial combats or beast hunts. Therefore ludi were connected to and held at the same time as Roman religious festivals and thus became part of a cult of the state. As religious ceremonies, ludi were organized at first by various colleges of priests; during the Republic, they were later presented by consuls. The close connections between games, play, ritual and politics should be noted – as almost always. After horse races theatrical plays soon became popular also: ludi scaenici, theatrical performances. The most popular of these plays were Roman type comedies, as in the works of Plautus – and here we return to the theme of the mask, for Plautus developed all his comedies around the notion of masks. The story is one of progressive growth in numbers of people attending and days of the year dedicated to play. By the late imperial era the annual plays had expanded to include the following list, which is far from exhaustive: • Ludi Megalenses, 4–10 April, established 204 bc in honour of the Magna Mater in conjunction with the Megalensia. • Ludi Ceriales, 12–19April, established 202 bc in conjunction with the Cerealia, April 12. • Ludi Florales, 28 April–3 May, established 173 bc in honour of Flora, in conjunction with the Floralia, May 1 and characterised by its ‘atmosphere of primitive license and pastoral orgy’. • Ludi Apollinares, July 6–13, first celebrated in 211 bc in honour of Apollo to secure his aid against Hannibal, and made annual in 208 bc by senatorial decree. • Ludi Romani, September 4–19 in 44 bc, September 12–15 in the fourth century ad, established according to some legends in the sixth century bc in honour of Jupiter and at first held occasionally, not annually. • Ludi Augustales, October 3–12, established ad 14 after the death of Augustus and based on the Augustalia. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 153 • Ludi Plebeii, originally November 13, on the Ides of Jupiter, and expanded to run November 4–17; established 216 bc and held in the Circus, and continued in the fourth century ad. To establish a fuller picture of the number of games within a given year, one should add to the list a whole series of games that were not held annually, but that were instead celebrated bi-annually or in some other cycle, plus a growing number of single-occasion ludi, often proposed by emperors to satisfy the population on an ad hoc basis. This intensified during the first century bc. It must be noted that there seems to exist a relationship between the fall of the Republic, the transition to empire and the proliferation of games. The proliferation of games was both a component and symptom of the decline and fall of the Roman Empire. As discussed by Szakolczai (2013, p. 95 ff), this relationship also extends to the sudden spread of pantomime, again testifying to the mimetic forces at play. The consolidation of this genre took place during the second half of the first century bc, and was simultaneous with the key periods in the establishment of permanent dictatorship and empire. When Rome was still functioning like a city-state, a certain degree of internal balance and harmony still reigned within the city. Citizenship was exercised with pride and respect for one’s society.
